type_of_website_required.details_about_type_of_website_required:
We are a small, family-run residential architecture studio based in Portland, Oregon, called "Northline Residential Design." The primary purpose of the new website is to function as a high-quality online brochure and portfolio that also generates qualified leads from homeowners planning major remodels, additions, and custom homes.

We do NOT need full ecommerce functionality (we don’t sell products online), but we do want the site to make it extremely easy for visitors to inquire about projects, schedule a consultation, and download our free planning guides in exchange for their email addresses.

So the site needs to be:
- A visually rich portfolio site that showcases our past projects with professional photography, project descriptions, and before/after comparisons.
- A lead-generation tool that includes clear calls to action ("Book a Free 20-Minute Call," "Download Our Remodeling Checklist," etc.), simple forms, and basic integration with our email marketing platform (we currently use Mailchimp but are open to switching if needed).
- An informational hub where prospective clients can learn about our process, pricing expectations, timelines, and what it’s like to work with a small architecture studio.

We would like separate sections for:
- Portfolio (filterable by project type: kitchen remodels, whole-house renovations, additions, ADUs, and custom homes).
- Services and process (step-by-step explanation of how we work from initial discovery call through permitting and construction documents).
- About (our story as a husband-and-wife team, our values, and our design philosophy).
- Resources (blog articles, downloadable PDFs, FAQs about working with an architect, and a glossary of common renovation terms).
- Contact (short and long contact forms, plus an option to directly book a video call via Calendly or a similar booking tool).

We do not need membership areas or login systems. A clean, modern, mobile-first site that loads quickly and is easy to manage on our end is the priority.

website_goals_and_requirements.details_about_what_we_need_our_new_websi:
Overall goals:
We want the new website to drive more (and better-qualified) inquiries for mid- to high-budget residential projects in the Portland metro area, while also reducing the amount of time we spend answering the same basic questions over and over via email and phone.

1) Increase sales and leads
- Our current annual average is about 3–4 new full-house or large-addition projects per year, mostly from word of mouth and referrals. We would like the new site to help us consistently generate 6–8 qualified project inquiries per month, with at least 2–3 of those being strong fits for our ideal project size (construction budgets of $250k+).
- We want clear calls to action on nearly every page (e.g., "Talk to an Architect," "Request a Feasibility Review"). These should take users to a short intake form that asks key questions (location, type of project, approximate budget, ideal start date) to help us pre-qualify leads.
- We’d like the option for a multi-step form or at least conditional fields so that people aren’t overwhelmed but we still collect enough info to know whether to schedule a call.

2) Provide better information to existing and prospective clients
- We spend a lot of time explaining the difference between an architect, designer, and contractor, as well as what our fees typically cover. The new site should host a series of clearly structured pages and/or blog posts that explain:
  - Our design process (discovery, schematic design, design development, construction documents, permitting, and support during construction).
  - Typical timelines for different project types (e.g., a kitchen remodel vs. a new ADU vs. a full custom home).
  - Budget ranges and cost expectations in plain language, with the necessary disclaimers.
  - Frequently asked questions (e.g., "Do I need a survey?", "Will you help with permits?", "Can you work with my contractor?").
- We’d also like a private but simple "Client Resources" page that we can share via link only (not indexed in navigation) where we can post PDFs like sample contracts, checklists, and guides for active clients.

3) Have a better, more professional design
- Our current site looks dated and doesn’t reflect the quality of our recent work. The new design should feel:
  - Clean, minimal, and modern, with a focus on large imagery and generous white space.
  - Consistent with our brand colors (soft forest green, charcoal, off-white) and our existing logo.
  - Highly visual on desktop and mobile, with large project photos, simple typography, and clear hierarchy.
- We’d like the homepage to immediately show our best work with a small, curated set of featured projects and a short tagline like: "Thoughtful residential architecture for real families in Portland."

4) Be easy to update in-house
- We are not very technical. We need a CMS where we can:
  - Add new projects (with photos, descriptions, and project details) without needing a developer.
  - Post new blog articles or resource pages.
  - Edit text on key pages if our process or pricing ranges change.
- Ideally, this would be built on WordPress or another widely used platform with a visual editor. We do not want something so custom that we are locked into one developer forever.
- We also want simple, clear training (written or short video walkthroughs) on how to:
  - Edit page content.
  - Upload and compress images.
  - Create new posts.
  - Update basic SEO fields (page title, meta description, image alt text).

5) Improve the customer experience
- The site should load quickly on mobile devices, since many of our leads find us while browsing on their phones or tablets.
- The navigation needs to be extremely clear and uncluttered. Right now, people tell us they can’t find our portfolio or don’t realize we do more than just kitchen remodels.
- We want a more intuitive structure, something like:
  - Home
  - Portfolio
  - Services & Process
  - About
  - Resources
  - Contact
- On project pages, we’d like visitors to be able to easily move between projects ("Next Project," "Previous Project") and also see related projects (for example, other ADUs or other whole-house renovations).
- We would also like to incorporate social proof more clearly—client testimonials, maybe a few short client stories, and any awards or publications (we were featured in a small local design blog and in a neighborhood magazine in 2023).

6) Capture and track more data for marketing
- We currently rely mostly on guesswork and word of mouth. We’d like the new site to be set up with:
  - Google Analytics 4 (GA4) to track traffic, page views, and basic user behavior.
  - Conversion tracking for key actions: form submissions, clicks on email addresses, and clicks to call our phone number from mobile devices.
  - Integration with an email marketing tool (currently Mailchimp; open to alternatives) so that people who download our guides are automatically added to a segmented email list.
- We’d like simple, easy-to-understand dashboards or at least a basic tutorial on how to check:
  - How many leads we’re getting from the website each month.
  - Which pages people are visiting before they contact us.
  - Which blog posts or resources are getting the most traffic.

7) Other specific needs
- Calendar integration: We use Calendly to schedule initial 20-minute discovery calls. It would be ideal if visitors could see available time slots and book directly from our site without a lot of friction.
- Image handling: Our photographer delivers large, high-resolution images. The site should be set up to handle image compression and scaling so that pages load quickly but photos still look sharp.
- Basic SEO foundations: We’re not looking for an intensive SEO campaign, but we do want the site built with best practices so we can rank locally over time for terms like "Portland residential architect," "Portland home remodel design," etc.
- Accessibility: We want the design to follow basic accessibility guidelines (legible font sizes, good color contrast, alt text for images, keyboard navigability) so that the site is usable by as many people as possible.

In summary, the new website needs to attract the right kind of clients, answer their main questions, make it easy for them to contact us or book a call, and give us better visibility into what is and isn’t working from a marketing perspective.

existing_website_feedback.things_we_like_and_dislike_about_our_exi:
Context: Our current website is www.northlineresidential.com (built in 2017 on an outdated WordPress theme by a friend). It has served us okay as a basic online presence, but it no longer reflects where our business is today.

Things we like about the existing website:
1) It exists and has our basic info:
   - It does at least give us a web address to put on business cards and email signatures, and people can find our phone number and a simple contact form. For years that was "good enough," especially when most of our work came from referrals.

2) Some of the older project photos are still good:
   - We have a few projects from 2018–2019 that are displayed reasonably well, and several clients have told us they first reached out because they recognized a style they liked from one of these projects.
   - We like that the site currently has a simple grid of projects on the portfolio page—no fancy filters, but at least everything is in one place.

3) The site is relatively simple and not overloaded with content:
   - There aren’t a million pages, which makes it less overwhelming to maintain and less confusing for visitors. We’d like to keep that sense of simplicity, even as we add more structure and resources.

Things we dislike about the existing website:
1) Outdated design and not mobile-friendly enough:
   - The theme feels very 2015: small fonts, cramped layout, and a lot of grey. The homepage hero image is low-resolution and looks blurry on newer screens.
   - On mobile, the menu can be hard to tap, and images don’t always resize properly. We’ve had two separate clients mention that they gave up trying to navigate our portfolio on their phones.

2) Confusing navigation and poor structure:
   - Our services, process, and pricing information are all buried inside one long "Services" page that very few people seem to read all the way through.
   - There is no clear distinction between "kitchen remodels," "whole-house renovations," "additions," and "ADUs," even though those are very different project types. People often email us asking whether we do ADUs because they can’t find that information easily.

3) Hard for us to update:
   - The site was built using a page builder plugin that we don’t fully understand. Every time we try to change text or add a new image, we worry about breaking the layout.
   - As a result, we haven’t added any new projects in almost two years, even though we have several beautiful finished projects we’d love to showcase.
   - The blog is basically dead—we posted 3 articles in 2018 and then stopped because it was too cumbersome to format things nicely.

4) Weak calls to action and lead capture:
   - The only real call to action is a small "Contact" link in the top navigation and a generic contact form on that page.
   - There’s no clear explanation of what happens after someone fills out the form, and we’re not asking any qualifying questions (budget, timeline, type of project), so we spend a lot of time responding to inquiries that are not a good fit.
   - We also don’t have any downloadable resources or ways to capture emails from people who are still in the early research phase.

5) No meaningful analytics or tracking:
   - We technically have Google Analytics installed, but it’s the old Universal Analytics setup, and we rarely log in. We don’t have goals or events configured, so we don’t know how many leads actually originate from the website.
   - We also can’t easily tell which pages people visit the most, how long they stay, or where they drop off.

6) Inconsistent branding and messaging:
   - The colors on the site don’t quite match the palette we now use on our printed materials and proposals.
   - The copy was written when we were just starting out, so it leans heavily on "We can do anything" rather than clearly stating that we specialize in residential remodels and additions for families in the Portland area.
   - We also recently updated our logo in 2023, but the website still uses the old version.

7) Limited social proof:
   - We have a few short testimonials buried on the About page, but they’re not very visible and don’t include photos or context.
   - We’ve been featured in a local design blog and a neighborhood magazine, but there’s no mention of that on the website.

In summary, we like that our existing website gives us a basic online presence and has a small portfolio, but it feels dated, is difficult for us to maintain, doesn’t communicate our niche clearly, and doesn’t actively help us attract or qualify the right kind of projects. We’re looking for a fresh start that builds on what worked (simplicity and strong imagery) but modernizes everything else, especially mobile usability, structure, branding, and lead generation.
